message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Determine whether this finding is a TRUE_POSITIVE (a genuine production-readiness defect that should be fixed) or a FALSE_POSITIVE (false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design, or not a real defect). Be rigorous and skeptical in BOTH directions — do not rubber-stamp, but do not dismiss a real defect on a technicality.

Out-of-scope-by-design exclusions (these are NOT defects): the dataset-agnostic engine receiving opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; transcripts intentionally committed (and scrubbed); on-box/loopback data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope limitations.

FINDING K-REL-1 (high): SIGTERM handler swallows process termination when prior disposition is SIG_DFL
Location: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py ~148-156 (_install_flush_on_exit._handler)
Claim: The installed SIGTERM handler flushes/shuts down the tracer then does `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. When no prior Python handler exists, prior == signal.SIG_DFL which is NOT callable, so the handler returns WITHOUT terminating — it replaced the default terminate disposition, so the process no longer dies on SIGTERM (docker stop / kill / k8s) until SIGKILL escalation. Tracing is on by default; bootstrap runs on the main thread in run/resume paths. The unit test only asserts flush/shutdown ran, never that termination still happens.

Read the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash) and, where the claim is empirically testable, REPRODUCE it (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ... ; use "python -m pytest" not bare pytest). Judge ONLY K-REL-1. Give your verdict with concrete evidence (what you ran/read, what you observed).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

136	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
137	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
138	
139	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
140	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
141	    """
142	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
143	    if not callable(flush):
144	        return
145	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
146	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
147	        try:
148	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
149	
150	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
151	                _safe(flush)
152	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
153	                if callable(prior):
154	                    prior(signum, frame)
155	
156	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
157	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
158	            pass

161	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
162	    try:
163	        fn()
164	    except Exception:
165	        pass

content.text: Confirmed: default SIGTERM disposition is `SIG_DFL` (0), which is not callable. Now let me reproduce the actual end-to-end behavior — that installing this handler prevents process termination on SIGTERM.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && cat > /tmp/repro_kre1.py <<'EOF'
import signal, os, time, sys

# Simulate the exact handler logic from tracing.py _install_flush_on_exit
prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
sys.stderr.write(f"prior={prior!r} callable={callable(prior)}\n")

def flush(): pass
class P:
    def shutdown(self): pass
provider = P()

def _safe(fn):
    try: fn()
    except Exception: pass

def _handler(signum, frame):
    _safe(flush)
    _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
    if callable(prior):
        prior(signum, frame)

signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
sys.stderr.write("handler installed; sending SIGTERM to self\n")
sys.stderr.flush()
os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM)
# If the handler swallowed termination, we reach here
sys.stderr.write("STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM (termination swallowed)\n")
sys.stderr.flush()
time.sleep(0.2)
sys.stderr.write("exiting normally\n")
EOF
python /tmp/repro_kre1.py; echo "EXIT CODE: $?"
input.description: Reproduce SIGTERM swallowing with replicated handler logic

content.content:
prior=<Handlers.SIG_DFL: 0> callable=False
handler installed; sending SIGTERM to self
STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM (termination swallowed)
exiting normally
EXIT CODE: 0

67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:

content.text:
Confirmed. The test monkeypatches `getsignal` to return `SIG_DFL` (line 211), then invokes the handler (line 228) and asserts only `shutdown >= 1`. It never asserts the process would still terminate. With prior == `SIG_DFL` (not callable), the handler returns normally and termination is swallowed — exactly the bug, and the test passes anyway.

I have enough evidence. Let me confirm the default Python behavior: that absent any installation, SIGTERM terminates (so the handler genuinely changes behavior).

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && cat > /tmp/repro_default.py <<'EOF'
import os, signal, time, sys
# No handler installed - default SIG_DFL should terminate
os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM)
sys.stderr.write("STILL ALIVE (should not reach)\n")
time.sleep(0.5)
EOF
python /tmp/repro_default.py; echo "EXIT CODE: $? (143 = 128+SIGTERM means terminated by signal)"
input.description: Confirm default SIGTERM terminates the process

content.content: EXIT CODE: 143 (143 = 128+SIGTERM means terminated by signal)

content.text:
The contrast is definitive:
- Default (no handler): SIGTERM terminates, exit 143.
- With the installed handler and `prior == SIG_DFL`: process survives, exit 0.

All elements of the claim are confirmed empirically.

input.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code and reproducing the behavior.

Code (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:146-156): on the main thread, _install_flush_on_exit captures `prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` and installs a handler that flushes/shuts down the tracer, then only re-raises to the prior handler via `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. It replaces SIGTERM's disposition unconditionally.

Empirical facts:
1. `signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` returns `signal.SIG_DFL` (value 0) when no Python handler is installed (the common production case), and `callable(SIG_DFL)` is False. So the `if callable(prior)` branch is skipped and the handler returns normally — termination is swallowed.
2. Reproduced with the exact handler logic: a process that installs the handler then sends itself SIGTERM prints "STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM (termination swallowed)" and exits 0.
3. Control: a process with no handler installed that sends itself SIGTERM dies with exit 143 (128+SIGTERM). This proves the installed handler genuinely changes behavior — the process that would have died now survives until SIGKILL escalation (e.g., docker stop's 10s grace, k8s terminationGracePeriod).

Reachability is real and on by default:
- Tracing is enabled unless ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING is set (_disabled, line 53-54), so the default path runs.
- bootstrap_tracing() -> _install_flush_on_exit() is called in the synchronous run path (pipeline.py:381) and resume/orchestration path (orchestration/graph.py:111,139). These execute on the caller's thread; for the CLI/run-study entry there is no thread/process pool spawning, so it is the main thread, satisfying the `threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()` guard at tracing.py:146.

Test gap confirmed: tests/test_observability.py:195-231 monkeypatches getsignal to return SIG_DFL (line 211), invokes the handler (line 228), and only asserts flush/shutdown ran (calls["shutdown"] >= 1). It never asserts the process would still terminate, so the test passes despite the swallowed-termination defect.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect (graceful-shutdown / container-stop semantics broken in the default configuration), not covered by any out-of-scope-by-design exclusion. A correct fix would re-raise the default disposition explicitly when prior is SIG_DFL (e.g., reset to SIG_DFL and re-deliver, or raise SystemExit / os.kill with default) and add a test asserting termination still occurs.

content.text: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The SIGTERM handler genuinely swallows process termination when the prior disposition is SIG_DFL (the default), confirmed both by reading the code and reproducing the behavior (handler installed → exit 0 after self-SIGTERM; no handler → exit 143). The bug is reachable on the default-enabled tracing path via the main-thread run/resume bootstrap, and the existing test does not catch it.
